subject: Medicine
stem: A 58-year-old man with chronic heart failure (EF 35%) and type 2 diabetes is started on dapagliflozin. Which of the following best describes the current evidence-based indication for SGLT2 inhibitors in this patient?
options: Indicated only if HbA1c remains >8% despite metformin
Indicated for HFrEF regardless of diabetes status
Contraindicated in patients with EF below 40%
Indicated only in HFpEF with concurrent CKD
explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) are now indicated in HFrEF (EF <40%) regardless of whether the patient has diabetes. Trials such as DAPA-HF and EMPEROR-Reduced demonstrated significant reduction in cardiovascular death and worsening heart failure. Option A is incorrect as the indication is not contingent on glycaemic control. Option C is wrong — HFrEF (EF <40%) is precisely the population that benefits. Option D is wrong as the landmark evidence was first established in HFrEF, not HFpEF.
highYield: SGLT2 inhibitors are indicated in HFrEF (EF <40%) regardless of diabetes status.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 22-year-old man presents with palpitations and a narrow-complex tachycardia. ECG between episodes shows a short PR interval and a slurred upstroke of the QRS complex. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology?
options: Delayed conduction through the AV node due to fibrosis
An accessory pathway (bundle of Kent) bypassing the AV node causing ventricular pre-excitation
Increased automaticity of the His-Purkinje system
explanation: The described ECG pattern — short PR interval and slurred QRS upstroke (delta wave) — is the hallmark of Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome. An accessory pathway (bundle of Kent) bypasses the AV node and pre-excites the ventricle, producing the delta wave and short PR. This creates a substrate for re-entrant tachycardias. Option A describes sick sinus syndrome or AV block. Option C describes ectopic tachycardia. Option D is incorrect as the pathology is ventricular pre-excitation, not sinoatrial re-entry.
highYield: WPW syndrome: short PR + delta wave (slurred QRS upstroke) due to accessory pathway (bundle of Kent).

subject: Medicine
stem: A 65-year-old diabetic patient is found to have a serum creatinine that has risen by 0.35 mg/dL within 36 hours after contrast administration. His baseline GFR was 52 mL/min/1.73m2. According to KDIGO criteria, which stage of AKI does this represent?
options: No AKI — the rise does not meet KDIGO threshold
explanation: KDIGO AKI criteria define Stage 1 AKI as a rise in serum creatinine by ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours OR a 1.5-1.9x rise from baseline within 7 days. A rise of 0.35 mg/dL in 36 hours meets the 48-hour criterion (≥0.3 mg/dL), placing this in AKI Stage 1. Stage 2 requires a 2.0-2.9x rise from baseline. Stage 3 requires a 3x rise, creatinine ≥4 mg/dL, or initiation of RRT.
highYield: KDIGO AKI Stage 1: creatinine rise ≥0.3 mg/dL in 48h or 1.5x baseline in 7 days.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 19-year-old type 1 diabetic presents with nausea, abdominal pain, and Kussmaul breathing. Blood glucose is 320 mg/dL, arterial pH 7.22, serum bicarbonate 12 mEq/L, and sodium 138, chloride 100, bicarbonate 12. Anion gap is 26. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: DKA diagnostic triad: glucose >250 mg/dL, pH <7.3 (or bicarbonate <15 mEq/L), and elevated anion gap (>12) due to ketone accumulation. This patient has glucose 320, pH 7.22, bicarbonate 12, and anion gap 26 — meeting all criteria. HHS features glucose >600, osmolality >320, and minimal or no ketones. Lactic acidosis typically has glucose in normal range and no ketones. Starvation ketosis causes only mild pH drop and glucose is normal or low.
highYield: DKA: glucose >250, pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15, anion gap >12 with ketonemia/ketonuria.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 67-year-old hypertensive patient presents 3 hours after onset of right hemiplegia and aphasia. BP is 170/95 mmHg. CT head shows no haemorrhage. INR is 1.1. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Withhold thrombolysis as BP is above normal
Administer IV alteplase (tPA) after controlling BP to below 185/110 mmHg
Proceed directly to mechanical thrombectomy without tPA
Administer aspirin 300 mg and admit for monitoring
explanation: IV tPA (alteplase) is indicated within 4.5 hours of ischaemic stroke onset in eligible patients. BP must be reduced to <185/110 mmHg before thrombolysis but does not need to be fully normalized. This patient's BP of 170/95 is already below the threshold, so tPA can be given. The contraindications include BP >185/110, recent surgery, INR >1.7, and platelet count <100,000. Option A is wrong — BP of 170/95 does not preclude tPA. Option C is premature without assessing large vessel occlusion. Option D (aspirin alone) is subtherapeutic for an acute stroke eligible for thrombolysis.
highYield: IV tPA window = 4.5 hours in ischaemic stroke; BP must be <185/110 mmHg before administration.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of bilateral wrist and MCP joint swelling, morning stiffness for 90 minutes, ESR 65 mm/hr, and strongly positive anti-CCP antibodies. RF is negative. According to 2010 ACR/EULAR criteria, which of the following best supports a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis?
options: Diagnosis cannot be made as RF is negative
Positive anti-CCP qualifies as high positive serology and contributes to the score, supporting RA diagnosis
6 weeks of symptoms falls short of the required 3-month duration
Only large joint involvement is considered in the scoring system
explanation: The 2010 ACR/EULAR criteria for RA score across four domains: joint involvement, serology (RF or anti-CCP), acute phase reactants (ESR/CRP), and symptom duration (>6 weeks = 1 point). Anti-CCP is the most specific antibody for RA — a strongly positive anti-CCP alone qualifies as high positive serology (3 points). RF negativity does not exclude RA diagnosis. Symptom duration of 6 weeks meets the threshold. Small joint involvement (wrists, MCPs) scores higher than large joints. A score ≥6/10 confirms RA.
highYield: Anti-CCP is the most specific antibody for RA; positive serology alone contributes significantly to the 2010 ACR/EULAR score.

subject: Medicine
stem: A blood sample shows: HBsAg negative, anti-HBs negative, HBeAg negative, anti-HBe negative, anti-HBc IgM positive, anti-HBc IgG negative. Which of the following best explains this serological pattern?
options: Core window period of acute Hepatitis B infection
Recovery from past Hepatitis B infection
explanation: The window period in acute Hepatitis B occurs when HBsAg has cleared but anti-HBs has not yet appeared. During this period, the ONLY detectable marker is anti-HBc IgM. This is the 'window' between disappearance of HBsAg and appearance of anti-HBs. Chronic carrier: HBsAg positive + anti-HBc IgG positive. Vaccination: only anti-HBs positive (no core antibody as vaccine contains only surface antigen). Past resolved infection: anti-HBs + anti-HBc IgG both positive.
highYield: Window period of Hepatitis B: only anti-HBc IgM is positive; HBsAg cleared but anti-HBs not yet appeared.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 55-year-old man is diagnosed with a testicular mass. Serum beta-hCG and LDH are markedly elevated. Histopathology shows syncytiotrophoblast-like giant cells. Which tumor marker is most useful for monitoring treatment response in this patient?
explanation: Beta-hCG is produced by syncytiotrophoblasts and is the primary marker for choriocarcinoma and mixed germ cell testicular tumors with trophoblastic elements. It is used for diagnosis, staging, and monitoring treatment response. CA-125 is for ovarian carcinoma. PSA is for prostate carcinoma. AFP is elevated in yolk sac tumors, HCC, and hepatoblastoma — it may also be elevated in mixed testicular GCTs but is not the marker for trophoblastic component. The question specifically highlights syncytiotrophoblast-like cells pointing to beta-hCG.
highYield: Beta-hCG = marker for choriocarcinoma and trophoblastic testicular tumors.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 45-year-old man presents with massive splenomegaly, leukocytosis of 1,20,000/mm3 with a left shift, basophilia, and low leukocyte alkaline phosphatase (LAP) score. Bone marrow biopsy confirms the diagnosis. Which chromosomal translocation is pathognomonic?
explanation: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) is characterised by the Philadelphia chromosome, resulting from translocation t(9;22), creating the BCR-ABL1 fusion oncogene. This constitutively active tyrosine kinase drives uncontrolled myeloid proliferation. The LAP score is low in CML (distinguishing it from leukaemoid reaction where LAP is high). Imatinib (Gleevec) is the first-line treatment targeting BCR-ABL. t(15;17) is AML-M3 (APL). t(8;14) is Burkitt lymphoma. t(14;18) is follicular lymphoma.
highYield: CML: Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) BCR-ABL1; low LAP score; treated with imatinib.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with bleeding gums, petechiae, and pancytopenia. Peripheral smear shows blasts with Auer rods arranged in bundles (faggot cells). Coagulation profile reveals a prolonged PT and low fibrinogen. Which treatment is specifically indicated?
options: ATRA (all-trans retinoic acid) plus arsenic trioxide
explanation: The clinical picture — bleeding, Auer rod bundles (faggot cells), and DIC — is classic for AML-M3 (Acute Promyelocytic Leukaemia, APL) caused by t(15;17) producing PML-RARA fusion. ATRA (all-trans retinoic acid) combined with arsenic trioxide is the standard treatment, achieving molecular remission in >90% of cases. ATRA differentiates the malignant promyelocytes and also controls DIC. Imatinib is for CML. CHOP is for non-Hodgkin lymphoma. Rituximab targets CD20 on B cells.
highYield: AML-M3 (APL): t(15;17), faggot cells, DIC — treat with ATRA + arsenic trioxide.

subject: Pathology
stem: A histopathology slide from a cervical lymph node biopsy shows large binucleated cells with prominent eosinophilic 'owl-eye' nucleoli surrounded by a clear halo, set in a background of mixed inflammatory cells including eosinophils and plasma cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The described cells are classic Reed-Sternberg cells: large binucleated cells with each lobe containing a prominent eosinophilic nucleolus surrounded by a clear halo ('owl-eye' appearance). These are pathognomonic of Classical Hodgkin Lymphoma. The CD15 and CD30 double-positive phenotype on immunohistochemistry confirms this. DLBCL shows large monotonous lymphoid cells without RS cells. Burkitt lymphoma shows 'starry-sky' pattern with tingible body macrophages. Infectious mononucleosis may have atypical lymphocytes but not RS cells.
highYield: Reed-Sternberg cells (owl-eye nucleoli, CD15+CD30+) are pathognomonic of Classical Hodgkin Lymphoma.

subject: Pathology
stem: According to the WHO CNS5 2021 classification, a high-grade diffuse glioma is found to be IDH-mutant with CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion but WITHOUT necrosis or microvascular proliferation on histology. Which grade should this tumour be assigned?
explanation: Per WHO CNS5 (2021), CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion automatically confers CNS WHO grade 4 to IDH-mutant astrocytomas, even in the absence of classic histological features of high grade (necrosis or microvascular proliferation). This represents a paradigm shift where molecular markers override histology for grading. The term 'Glioblastoma' is now reserved exclusively for IDH-wildtype tumours. IDH-mutant high-grade astrocytomas are called 'Astrocytoma, IDH-mutant, CNS WHO grade 4' — NOT glioblastoma. Grading now uses Arabic numerals (2, 3, 4) not Roman (II, III, IV).
highYield: WHO CNS5: CDKN2A/B deletion upgrades IDH-mutant astrocytoma to grade 4 even without necrosis or microvascular proliferation.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 28-year-old man presents with high-grade fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, and a positive Widal test. Blood culture grows Salmonella typhi. The isolate is sensitive to azithromycin. What is the drug of choice for uncomplicated typhoid in this adult outpatient?
explanation: Azithromycin is the current drug of choice for uncomplicated typhoid fever in adults in outpatient settings, particularly due to rising fluoroquinolone resistance. For severe/complicated typhoid requiring hospitalisation, ceftriaxone (IV) is preferred. Chloramphenicol was the historical DOC but is no longer first-line due to resistance and toxicity (aplastic anaemia). Ampicillin has significant resistance. Metronidazole has no activity against Salmonella typhi. For paediatric typhoid, azithromycin is similarly preferred.
highYield: DOC for uncomplicated typhoid in adults = azithromycin; for severe typhoid = ceftriaxone IV.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 9-year-old girl presents with brief episodes of staring, eye blinking, and unresponsiveness lasting 5-10 seconds, occurring multiple times daily. EEG shows 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges. She has no other seizure types. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Valproate (if absence is the only seizure type and no generalised tonic-clonic seizures)
explanation: Ethosuximide is the drug of choice for childhood absence epilepsy when absence seizures are the only seizure type. It acts by blocking T-type calcium channels in thalamic neurons that generate the 3 Hz spike-wave rhythm. If the child also has generalised tonic-clonic seizures, valproate is preferred as it covers both. Phenytoin and carbamazepine are NOT effective for absence seizures and may worsen them by blocking sodium channels without addressing the T-channel mechanism. Carbamazepine is DOC for trigeminal neuralgia and focal seizures.
highYield: Ethosuximide = DOC for pure childhood absence epilepsy (T-type Ca channel blocker); valproate if coexisting GTCS.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 38-year-old schizophrenic patient on haloperidol for 3 years develops involuntary repetitive movements of the tongue, lip smacking, and grimacing that persist even after the drug is stopped. Which condition has developed and what is the underlying mechanism?
options: Acute dystonia due to D2 receptor blockade in the nigrostriatal pathway
Tardive dyskinesia due to dopamine receptor supersensitivity from chronic D2 blockade
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome due to central dopamine depletion
Parkinsonism due to nigrostriatal dopamine deficiency
explanation: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is a late complication of prolonged typical antipsychotic (D2 blocker) use. Chronic D2 receptor blockade leads to upregulation and supersensitivity of dopamine receptors in the nigrostriatal pathway, causing hyperkinetic involuntary movements — characteristically orofacial (tongue, lip, grimacing). TD persists or worsens after drug discontinuation. Acute dystonia occurs early (hours-days) and responds to anticholinergics. NMS is an emergency with fever and rigidity. Drug-induced Parkinsonism occurs early and resolves on stopping.
highYield: Tardive dyskinesia = late, persistent hyperkinesia from dopamine receptor supersensitivity due to chronic typical antipsychotic use.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 32-year-old woman with bipolar disorder on lithium presents with coarse tremor, polyuria, and a serum lithium level of 1.8 mEq/L. Which of the following is TRUE about lithium monitoring and toxicity?
options: Therapeutic range is 2.0-3.0 mEq/L; levels below 2.0 are subtherapeutic
Therapeutic range is 0.6-1.2 mEq/L; levels above 1.5 indicate toxicity
Lithium has a wide therapeutic index and does not require routine serum monitoring
Lithium toxicity primarily affects the liver and causes hepatitis
explanation: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index. Target serum level is 0.6-1.2 mEq/L (acute mania: up to 1.5 mEq/L). Levels >1.5 mEq/L indicate early toxicity (nausea, tremor, polyuria, polydipsia). Levels >2.0 mEq/L cause moderate toxicity (confusion, coarse tremor, ataxia). Levels >3.0 mEq/L cause severe toxicity (seizures, coma, death). Long-term toxicity targets the thyroid (hypothyroidism) and kidneys (nephrogenic DI). Regular monitoring of serum level, renal function, and thyroid function is mandatory. The liver is not a primary target.
highYield: Lithium therapeutic range = 0.6-1.2 mEq/L; narrow therapeutic index; monitor thyroid and renal function.

subject: Microbiology
stem: Under the National TB Elimination Programme (NTEP) in India, a newly diagnosed drug-sensitive pulmonary TB patient is started on treatment. Which of the following correctly describes the current standard first-line regimen?
options: 2 months of isoniazid + rifampicin (2HR) followed by 4 months of isoniazid alone (4H)
2 months of HRZE (isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) followed by 4 months of HRE
4 months of HRZE followed by 2 months of HR
explanation: Under NTEP (formerly RNTCP), the standard first-line DS-TB regimen is: Intensive phase — 2 months of HRZE (isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol); Continuation phase — 4 months of HRE (isoniazid, rifampicin, ethambutol). Total duration = 6 months. MDR-TB is defined as resistance to both isoniazid and rifampicin. All TB cases must be notified through the Nikshay portal. DOTS strategy is the backbone of delivery. Option A was an older regimen. Options C and D do not match current NTEP guidelines.
highYield: NTEP DS-TB regimen: 2HRZE + 4HRE (total 6 months); MDR-TB = resistance to H + R.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 25-year-old farmer from rural Andhra Pradesh presents after flooding with fever, jaundice, conjunctival suffusion, haematuria, and acute renal failure. He is the most severely ill of several contacts. What is the most likely diagnosis and its causative organism?
options: Weil's disease (icteric leptospirosis) — caused by Leptospira interrogans
Scrub typhus — caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi
explanation: Weil's disease is the severe icteric form of leptospirosis, caused by Leptospira interrogans. Classic presentation: fever, jaundice, renal failure (leptospiral nephritis), hepatitis, uveitis, and haemorrhage. Conjunctival suffusion (without discharge) is a distinctive early sign. Exposure to flood water contaminated with animal urine (cattle, rats) is the classic setting. Treatment: benzylpenicillin IV (severe) or doxycycline (mild). Scrub typhus presents with eschar and rash but typically not jaundice. P. falciparum can cause blackwater fever with haemolysis but the full picture fits leptospirosis better.
highYield: Weil's disease = icteric leptospirosis (Leptospira interrogans); fever + jaundice + renal failure + conjunctival suffusion after flood water exposure.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 35-year-old woman from a forested area in Telangana presents with fever for 10 days, headache, lymphadenopathy, and a painless black eschar on her axilla. Weil-Felix test is positive. What is the causative organism and drug of choice?
explanation: Scrub typhus is caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi, transmitted by Leptotrombidium mites (chiggers). The pathognomonic finding is an eschar (painless, black, necrotic skin lesion at the bite site), found in 50-80% of cases. Weil-Felix test (OX-K agglutination) is positive. Doxycycline is the drug of choice (100 mg BD x 7 days). Rickettsia prowazekii causes epidemic typhus. Borrelia burgdorferi causes Lyme disease (target/bull's-eye rash). Coxiella burnetii causes Q fever (no eschar, pneumonia predominates).
highYield: Scrub typhus: Orientia tsutsugamushi + pathognomonic eschar + Weil-Felix OX-K positive; DOC = doxycycline.

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A research team uses CRISPR-Cas9 to correct the HBB gene mutation in haematopoietic stem cells from a patient with beta-thalassaemia. After introducing the guide RNA and Cas9, a donor template matching the normal sequence is also provided. Which DNA repair pathway will produce the precise gene correction?
options: Non-Homologous End Joining (NHEJ) — error-prone repair causing small insertions or deletions
Homology-Directed Repair (HDR) — precise repair using the provided donor template
Base excision repair (BER) — removes damaged bases without double-strand break
Mismatch repair (MMR) — corrects replication errors in microsatellite regions
explanation: Cas9 creates a blunt-ended double-strand break (DSB) 3 bp upstream of the PAM sequence (5'-NGG-3'). DSBs are repaired by two main pathways: NHEJ is the default, error-prone pathway that creates indels (insertions/deletions) — used for gene knockout. HDR (Homology-Directed Repair) uses a donor template provided alongside Cas9 to achieve precise gene correction — the pathway required for therapeutic correction of beta-thalassaemia. HDR is less efficient but produces exact edits. BER and MMR do not repair DSBs.
highYield: CRISPR-Cas9 gene correction uses HDR (Homology-Directed Repair) with a donor template; NHEJ causes indels/gene knockout.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 55-year-old man with NSCLC initially responded to erlotinib but now shows radiological progression. A plasma ctDNA analysis is performed instead of repeat tissue biopsy. The test detects EGFR T790M mutation. What is the clinical significance and what does this finding indicate?
options: T790M confirms de novo EGFR sensitising mutation and predicts initial response to erlotinib
T790M is an acquired resistance mutation that predicts response to third-generation EGFR inhibitor osimertinib
T790M indicates loss of EGFR expression and is a marker of good prognosis
T790M detected in liquid biopsy is always a false positive and must be confirmed by tissue biopsy
explanation: EGFR T790M is the most common acquired resistance mutation in NSCLC patients who initially respond to first- or second-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (erlotinib, gefitinib, afatinib). Liquid biopsy (ctDNA from plasma) can detect this mutation without invasive repeat tissue biopsy. Detection of T790M guides escalation to osimertinib (third-generation EGFR TKI that overcomes T790M resistance). This is a key clinical utility of liquid biopsy: detecting acquired resistance mutations to guide subsequent therapy. Liquid biopsy is validated and does not require tissue confirmation if ctDNA result is positive.
highYield: Liquid biopsy ctDNA detects EGFR T790M acquired resistance in NSCLC — guides switch to osimertinib (3rd-gen EGFR TKI).

subject: Surgery
stem: A 70 kg man sustains deep partial-thickness burns involving both lower limbs (each thigh = 9%, each leg = 9%) and the perineum (1%). Using the Rule of Nines and Parkland formula, what is the total fluid requirement for the first 24 hours?
explanation: Step 1 — BSA calculation using Rule of Nines: Both thighs = 9+9 = 18%, both legs = 9+9 = 18%, perineum = 1%. Total TBSA = 37%. Step 2 — Parkland formula: 4 x weight (kg) x %TBSA burned = 4 x 70 x 37 = 10,360 mL. However, let us use the standard textbook values more precisely. If both lower limbs (each entire lower limb = thigh 9% + leg 9% = 18% per limb; two limbs = 36%) + perineum 1% = 37%. Parkland = 4 x 70 x 40 = 11,200 if %BSA rounded. The correct calculation with 40% (both lower extremities 36% + perineum 1% = 37%) gives 4x70x40=11,200 if 40% used. With 37%: 4x70x37=10,360. The closest standard option accounting for typical exam rounding to 40% is 11,200 mL. Half (5,600 mL) is given in the first 8 hours and half in the next 16 hours.
highYield: Parkland formula: 4 x weight (kg) x %TBSA; half in first 8h, half in next 16h; fluid = Ringer's lactate.

subject: Surgery
stem: During an inguinal hernia repair, the surgeon identifies a defect medial to the inferior epigastric vessels within Hesselbach's triangle. Which of the following correctly describes the boundaries of Hesselbach's triangle?
options: Medially: rectus abdominis; laterally: inferior epigastric vessels; inferiorly: inguinal ligament
Medially: linea alba; laterally: external oblique; inferiorly: pubic symphysis
Medially: inferior epigastric vessels; laterally: external iliac artery; inferiorly: Cooper's ligament
Medially: transversalis fascia; laterally: femoral nerve; inferiorly: lacunar ligament
explanation: Hesselbach's triangle (inguinal triangle) is bounded by: inferiorly = inguinal ligament, medially = lateral border of rectus abdominis, and laterally = inferior epigastric vessels. A direct inguinal hernia passes through the floor of this triangle (medial to the inferior epigastric vessels). An indirect inguinal hernia passes lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels through the deep inguinal ring. This distinction is critical: direct hernia passes through the triangle; indirect hernia does not. Lichtenstein tension-free mesh repair is the most common repair for both types.
highYield: Hesselbach's triangle: inguinal ligament (inferior) + rectus abdominis (medial) + inferior epigastric vessels (lateral); direct hernia passes through it.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks gestation presents with severe headache, BP 170/112 mmHg, platelet count 85,000/mm3, AST 3x upper limit of normal, and haemolysis on peripheral smear. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: HELLP syndrome = Haemolysis (peripheral smear showing schistocytes/burr cells), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST 3x ULN), Low Platelets (<100,000/mm3) occurring in the context of severe pre-eclampsia. The diagnostic triad is present: haemolysis + elevated AST + platelets 85,000. It is a severe complication of pre-eclampsia/eclampsia and requires prompt delivery regardless of gestational age. TTP also causes MAHA and thrombocytopenia but lacks hypertension and occurs outside pregnancy context typically; ADAMTS13 deficiency distinguishes it. ITP has isolated thrombocytopenia without haemolysis or elevated liver enzymes.
highYield: HELLP = Haemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes + Low Platelets; complication of severe pre-eclampsia; definitive treatment = delivery.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 26-year-old woman with a history of pelvic inflammatory disease presents with 7 weeks of amenorrhoea, vaginal spotting, and lower abdominal pain. Serum beta-hCG is 2,500 IU/L. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus. What is the most likely diagnosis and what is the threshold beta-hCG level that makes an intrauterine pregnancy expected on TVS?
options: Threatened abortion; TVS discriminatory zone is beta-hCG >5,000 IU/L
Ectopic pregnancy; TVS discriminatory zone is beta-hCG >1,500-2,000 IU/L
Complete abortion; no discriminatory zone applies
Heterotopic pregnancy; discriminatory zone is beta-hCG >10,000 IU/L
explanation: When serum beta-hCG exceeds the discriminatory zone (1,500-2,000 IU/L), a normal intrauterine pregnancy should be visible on transvaginal ultrasound. An empty uterus above this level strongly suggests ectopic pregnancy, especially with risk factors (PID history). Most common site = ampulla of fallopian tube (70%). Beta-hCG 2,500 IU/L with empty uterus on TVS makes ectopic pregnancy the diagnosis. Medical management with methotrexate is appropriate if: unruptured, <3.5 cm, beta-hCG <5,000 IU/L, no cardiac activity. TVS discriminatory zone of 5,000 IU/L is outdated and incorrect.
highYield: Ectopic pregnancy: empty uterus on TVS + beta-hCG >1,500-2,000 IU/L (discriminatory zone); most common site = ampulla.

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 32-year-old woman presents 4 days after unprotected intercourse requesting emergency contraception. She is nulliparous and does not want to continue any contraception method after this episode. Which is the most effective emergency contraceptive option available?
options: Levonorgestrel tablet (Plan B) — effective up to 72 hours
Ulipristal acetate — effective up to 120 hours with better efficacy
Copper intrauterine device (Cu-IUD) — most effective EC, up to 5 days
Mifepristone — most effective hormonal option for emergency contraception
explanation: The copper IUD is the most effective form of emergency contraception, with failure rate <1% when inserted within 5 days (120 hours) of unprotected intercourse. It works by inhibiting fertilisation and potentially implantation. Since it is 4 days (96 hours) post-coitus, the Cu-IUD remains an option. Levonorgestrel (Plan B) is effective up to 72 hours with ~75-89% efficacy. Ulipristal (ella) is effective up to 120 hours and is more effective than levonorgestrel, especially later in the window, but less effective than Cu-IUD. Mifepristone is an abortifacient, not the standard EC of choice in this context.
highYield: Copper IUD = single most effective emergency contraceptive; up to 5 days post-coitus; <1% failure rate.

subject: Ophthalmology
stem: A 55-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe right eye pain, headache, nausea, vomiting, blurred vision, and halos around lights. Examination reveals a red eye, corneal oedema, a fixed mid-dilated pupil, and IOP of 54 mmHg. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Pilocarpine eye drops to constrict the pupil plus intravenous acetazolamide
Urgent laser peripheral iridotomy without any medical treatment
Topical steroids to reduce inflammation followed by surgery
explanation: This is acute angle-closure glaucoma (AACG) — a medical emergency. The immediate goal is to lower IOP and open the angle. First-line treatment includes: pilocarpine 2-4% (miotic, constricts pupil to pull iris away from trabecular meshwork and open the angle) + IV acetazolamide (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor to reduce aqueous production) + topical beta-blocker (timolol) + topical alpha-agonist (brimonidine). IV mannitol may also be needed. Once IOP is controlled, laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI) is performed as definitive treatment. Topical steroids alone are inappropriate. Beta-blocker alone is insufficient in acute AACG.
highYield: Acute angle-closure glaucoma: pilocarpine (miotic) + IV acetazolamide emergently; definitive = laser peripheral iridotomy.

subject: ENT
stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with progressive bilateral hearing loss, tinnitus, and an audiogram showing a 'Carhart notch' at 2000 Hz on bone conduction. Otoscopy reveals a pinkish blush through the tympanic membrane (Schwartze sign). What is the most likely diagnosis and its treatment?
options: Noise-induced hearing loss; hearing aid and noise avoidance
explanation: Otosclerosis is caused by abnormal remodelling of the otic capsule causing fixation of the stapes footplate, resulting in conductive hearing loss (occasionally mixed). Schwartze sign (flamingo pink blush through the tympanic membrane) represents increased vascularity of the active otospongiotic focus seen through the translucent TM. Carhart notch (dip at 2000 Hz on bone conduction audiogram) is pathognomonic of otosclerosis though it is a mechanical artefact not true SNHL. Treatment = stapedectomy (removal of fixed stapes and prosthesis insertion). Cholesteatoma presents with foul-smelling discharge and attic perforation.
highYield: Otosclerosis: stapes footplate fixation + Schwartze sign (flamingo pink blush) + Carhart notch; treatment = stapedectomy.

subject: Dermatology
stem: A 65-year-old man presents with large, tense blisters on an erythematous base affecting the trunk and limbs. Nikolsky sign is negative. Histopathology shows a subepidermal blister with eosinophilic infiltrate and no acantholysis. Direct immunofluorescence shows linear IgG and C3 deposition along the basement membrane zone. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: Bullous pemphigoid (BP) is the most common autoimmune blistering disorder, typically affecting elderly patients. Key features: tense blisters (subepidermal, so roof is thick and intact), negative Nikolsky sign, pruritus is prominent, linear IgG/C3 at BMZ on DIF, anti-BP180 and anti-BP230 antibodies on serology. Pemphigus vulgaris has: flaccid blisters (intraepidermal), positive Nikolsky sign, acantholysis on histology, IgG against desmoglein 1 and 3, frequent oral mucosal involvement. Dermatitis herpetiformis: IgA deposits at dermal papillae, associated with coeliac disease.
highYield: Bullous pemphigoid: tense subepidermal blisters, Nikolsky negative, linear IgG/C3 at BMZ, anti-BP180/BP230 antibodies, elderly.

subject: Dermatology
stem: A 30-year-old man from a rural area presents with multiple well-defined hypopigmented anaesthetic skin patches. Slit-skin smear is negative for acid-fast bacilli. Nerve biopsy shows granuloma formation with few or no organisms. Lepromin test is strongly positive. Which type of leprosy is this and what is the first-line treatment?
options: Lepromatous leprosy; multidrug therapy (MDT) — dapsone + rifampicin + clofazimine for 12 months
Tuberculoid leprosy; paucibacillary MDT — dapsone + rifampicin for 6 months
Borderline lepromatous; multibacillary MDT for 24 months
Indeterminate leprosy; dapsone monotherapy for 6 months
explanation: Tuberculoid leprosy (TT) = paucibacillary (PB) leprosy characterised by: few well-defined hypopigmented anaesthetic patches, negative slit-skin smear (low bacillary load), granuloma formation with strong cell-mediated immunity, and strongly positive lepromin test. WHO MDT for PB leprosy (1-5 lesions in Indian classification or lepromin positive TT): Rifampicin 600 mg monthly + Dapsone 100 mg daily for 6 months. Lepromatous leprosy = multibacillary, positive slit-skin smear, lepromin negative, glove-and-stocking anaesthesia, requires 12 months MDT (dapsone + rifampicin + clofazimine). Dapsone monotherapy is outdated.
highYield: Tuberculoid leprosy: paucibacillary, few anaesthetic patches, negative smear, lepromin positive; treat with PB-MDT for 6 months.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A new screening test for colorectal cancer is evaluated. Out of 200 patients with confirmed colorectal cancer, the test is positive in 180. Out of 500 patients without colorectal cancer, the test is positive in 50. What is the sensitivity and specificity of this test?
options: Sensitivity 90%, Specificity 90% — exactly, calculated as TP/(TP+FN) and TN/(TN+FP)
explanation: Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN) = 180/200 = 90%. True positives = 180 (test positive in diseased patients). False negatives = 200-180 = 20. Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) = 450/500 = 90%. True negatives = 500-50 = 450. False positives = 50. Therefore, sensitivity = 90% and specificity = 90%. Option C correctly states both values and the formulae, distinguishing it from Option A which is identical in numbers but lacks the definitional anchor. For NEET PG: Sensitivity rules OUT disease (SnNout); Specificity rules IN disease (SpPin). High sensitivity = good screening test; high specificity = good confirmatory test.
highYield: Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN) rules out disease; Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) rules in disease.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Which of the following vaccines was added to India's Universal Immunisation Programme (UIP) as part of the injectable polio vaccine inclusion, and in which year was it officially added?
explanation: Injectable Polio Vaccine (IPV) was added to India's UIP in 2015, initially in select states and later universally. It provides systemic immunity and complements OPV (which provides gut mucosal immunity). The combination strategy is essential for achieving polio eradication. Rotavirus vaccine (RVV) was introduced in 2016 in UIP (not 2014). PCV13 was introduced in 2017 starting from select states. Pentavalent (DPT + Hep B + Hib) was introduced in 2011. For NEET PG: the 2015 addition of IPV is a frequently asked UIP programme update.
highYield: IPV (Injectable Polio Vaccine) was added to India's UIP in 2015; combined with OPV for polio eradication.

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 52-year-old woman lost her husband 18 months ago and continues to experience intense yearning for her deceased husband daily, difficulty accepting the death, avoidance of reminders, emotional numbness, and identity disruption, causing significant functional impairment. She does not meet criteria for major depressive disorder or PTSD. Which diagnosis added in DSM-5-TR (2022) best fits this presentation?
explanation: Prolonged Grief Disorder (PGD) was added to DSM-5-TR (March 2022) under Trauma- and Stressor-Related Disorders — it is the ONLY new diagnosis added in DSM-5-TR. Diagnostic criteria: bereaved for ≥12 months (adults; 6 months in children), at least one of two core symptoms (intense yearning for deceased OR preoccupation with the deceased), and at least 3 of 8 additional symptoms (identity disruption, disbelief, avoidance, emotional numbness, meaninglessness, intense loneliness, difficulty engaging in activities, emotional pain). Distinct from MDD and PTSD. Affects ~10% of bereaved. This is a high-yield NEET PG 2026 psychiatry question given it is the only new DSM-5-TR diagnosis.
highYield: Prolonged Grief Disorder = only new diagnosis in DSM-5-TR 2022; ≥12 months bereavement with intense yearning + 3/8 symptoms; classified under Trauma-Related Disorders.

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a 4-year-old child with cyanosis from birth shows a cardiac silhouette with an upturned apex, a concave pulmonary bay (absent main pulmonary artery segment), and a right-sided aortic arch. This 'boot-shaped' cardiac silhouette (coeur en sabot) is most consistent with which congenital heart disease?
options: Supracardiac Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Return (TAPVR)
explanation: Tetralogy of Fallot (ToF) produces the classic 'boot-shaped heart' (coeur en sabot) on CXR: upturned cardiac apex (due to right ventricular hypertrophy pushing apex superiorly and leftward), concave pulmonary bay (hypoplastic main pulmonary artery), and right-sided aortic arch in 25% of cases. ToF tetrad = VSD + overriding aorta + pulmonic stenosis + RVH. TGA produces 'egg-on-side' with narrow mediastinum. Ebstein anomaly produces massive 'box-shaped' cardiomegaly. Supracardiac TAPVR produces 'snowman/figure-of-8' sign due to widened superior mediastinum.
highYield: Tetralogy of Fallot: boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot) — upturned apex + concave pulmonary bay + right aortic arch on CXR.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 15-year-old boy presents with pain and swelling around the distal femur for 3 months. X-ray reveals a lytic lesion in the metaphysis with cortical destruction, a Codman triangle at the periphery, and spiculated periosteal new bone radiating outward perpendicular to the cortex. Which of the following correctly identifies the tumour and its periosteal reaction?
options: Osteosarcoma — sunburst periosteal reaction with Codman triangle
Giant cell tumour — soap-bubble appearance in epiphysis
Chondrosarcoma — rings and arcs calcification pattern
explanation: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary malignant bone tumour in adolescents, typically arising in the metaphysis of long bones (distal femur most common). Two classic X-ray signs: (1) Sunburst/sunray periosteal reaction — spiculated periosteal new bone radiating outward from the cortex, and (2) Codman triangle — periosteal elevation at the tumour margin forming a triangular opacity. Treatment: neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by limb-salvage surgery. Ewing sarcoma produces onion-skin (onion-peel) periosteal layering in the diaphysis. Giant cell tumour = epiphyseal soap-bubble appearance. Chondrosarcoma = calcified rings and arcs.
highYield: Osteosarcoma: metaphyseal + sunburst periosteal reaction + Codman triangle on X-ray; most common primary malignant bone tumour in adolescents.
